title: Fitting a pressure-reducing valve to a mains supply
subtitle: Install a PRV on an incoming mains supply pipe to reduce water pressure to a safe working range for appliances and fittings
excerpt: How to fit a pressure-reducing valve to a domestic mains supply. Covers where to fit the PRV in the supply line, making compression or push-fit connections, setting the outlet pressure, and checking with a test gauge.
sourceNotes: UK plumbing trade practice. Water Supply (Water Fittings) Regulations 1999 (requirements for appliance pressure limits. Caleffi and Honeywell generic PRV installation guidance (referenced principles, not brand instructions).

glossaryTerms.definition: A valve that automatically reduces a higher incoming water pressure to a preset lower outlet pressure. Mains supply pressure in the UK typically runs at 3 to 10 bar; most domestic appliances, fittings, and system boilers prefer 1.5 to 3 bar. Exceeding 3.5 bar causes push-fit fittings to leak, washing machine hoses to fail, and tap seats to wear quickly. A PRV is fitted on the incoming mains pipe before the first branch; it contains an internal spring and diaphragm that regulate pressure without a power supply. Most PRVs have an adjustment screw to set the outlet pressure and a test port for a pressure gauge.

content.text: stops high mains water pressure from damaging taps, joints, and appliances. Cut into the supply pipe after the main stopcock, fit the valve the right way round, and set the outlet pressure with the adjustment screw.

Connect a test pressure gauge to the cold tap nearest the stopcock. Open the tap fully and read the supply pressure. If the reading is consistently above 3 bar, a PRV is warranted. Readings above 5 bar are common in lower-elevation urban areas and cause push-fit fittings to drip.

Fit the PRV on the mains supply pipe immediately after the main stopcock and before any branch to the kitchen cold, garden tap, or water softener. The valve body is directional) note the flow-direction arrow and fit it correctly. Leave at least 200 mm of straight pipe upstream and downstream for the valve to work cleanly.

Turn off the mains stopcock and drain the pipe. Cut out a section of pipe to accept the PRV body: the exact removal length depends on the valve model. Fit compression fittings on both cut ends. Apply PTFE tape to all threaded ports on the valve and make up the compression unions finger-tight, then one-quarter turn with the spanner. Do not overtighten compression olives.
Set the outlet pressure and test

Restore the supply. Remove the cap on the PRV adjustment screw and turn clockwise to increase outlet pressure, anti-clockwise to decrease. Set to 2 to 2.5 bar. Check the gauge on the downstream side of the PRV. Check all connections for leaks. Replace the cap.
